name: Digital Literacy
description: An essential course developing the skills to navigate, evaluate, create, and communicate in digital environments, covering internet safety, cybersecurity, productivity tools, AI literacy, and digital citizenship.

modules.title: Introduction to Digital Literacy
modules.objectives: Define digital literacy and its importance in modern life; identify the key components of digital literacy; understand how digital skills impact education, employment, and citizenship.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — What Is Digital Literacy?
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Digital Literacy in the 21st Century
modules.assignment: Write a one-page personal digital audit: list the digital tools you use weekly, categorize them (communication, learning, entertainment, productivity), and reflect on which skills you would most like to improve.

modules.title: Navigating the Internet Safely
modules.objectives: Identify risks of online activity including scams, phishing, and malicious websites; use safe browsing practices; understand HTTPS, URLs, and how to verify website legitimacy.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Stay Safe Online
modules.assignment: Examine three websites (one legitimate, one suspicious, one clearly fake); use a checklist of trust indicators (HTTPS, domain age, contact info, grammar, design) to evaluate each and write a brief analysis of how you identified each.

modules.title: Evaluating Online Information
modules.objectives: Apply the SIFT method to evaluate digital information; identify misinformation, disinformation, and clickbait; fact-check claims using lateral reading and trusted fact-checking tools.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Spot Fake News
modules.assignment: Find three viral social media posts making factual claims; use lateral reading and fact-checking sites (Snopes, FactCheck.org) to verify or debunk each claim; write a report on your findings and what made each post believable or not.

modules.title: Online Privacy & Data
modules.objectives: Understand what personal data is collected online and how it is used; adjust privacy settings on common platforms; understand cookies, tracking, and data brokers; know your digital rights.
modules.assignment: Conduct a personal privacy audit: review and adjust the privacy settings on three apps or social media platforms you use; write a summary of what data each was collecting and what changes you made.

modules.title: Cybersecurity Fundamentals
modules.objectives: Describe common cyber threats including phishing, malware, ransomware, and social engineering; create strong passwords and use multi-factor authentication; understand how to protect personal and organizational data.
modules.assignment: Evaluate the security of five accounts you use regularly (school email, social media, banking app, game account, shopping site); grade each on password strength, MFA, and recovery options; create a personal cybersecurity improvement plan.

modules.title: Productivity Software: Docs, Sheets, Slides
modules.titleZh: 生产力软件：文档、表格、演示
modules.objectives: Create, format, and collaborate on documents using Google Docs or Microsoft Word; build basic spreadsheets with formulas; design clear presentation slides; use comments, sharing, and version history.
modules.readingNote: GCFGlobal — Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides Tutorials
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Google Sheets and Docs Introduction
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Google Workspace for Students Tutorial
modules.assignment: Create a research summary document in Google Docs with proper headings and citations; build a simple budget spreadsheet in Google Sheets using SUM, AVERAGE, and IF formulas; design a three-slide presentation in Google Slides summarizing a topic of your choice.

modules.title: Digital Communication & Netiquette
modules.objectives: Write effective professional emails; understand netiquette rules for online communication; navigate online collaboration tools (Slack, Teams, Google Meet); recognize and avoid cyberbullying.
modules.assignment: Write three emails: one to a teacher requesting an extension, one to a potential employer asking about an internship, and one to a classmate coordinating a group project; apply professional email conventions to each.

modules.title: Digital Research Skills
modules.objectives: Use advanced Google search operators; find scholarly sources using Google Scholar and library databases; evaluate source credibility using SIFT; organize digital research with citation management tools.
modules.readingNote: GCFGlobal — How to Do Research Online
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Use Google Search Like a Pro
modules.assignment: Research a health topic using at least four different search strategies (general Google, Google Scholar, library database, Wikipedia for leads only); compare the quality and depth of results from each approach.

modules.title: Copyright, Creative Commons & Fair Use
modules.titleZh: 版权、知识共享与合理使用
modules.objectives: Explain copyright and intellectual property basics; describe Creative Commons licenses and how to find free-to-use content; apply the four factors of fair use to academic scenarios.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Copyright and Fair Use Explained for Students
modules.assignment: Find five images online for a hypothetical school project; for each, identify its copyright status, the appropriate license, and whether you can use it freely, must attribute it, or cannot use it at all.

modules.title: Social Media Literacy
modules.objectives: Analyze how social media algorithms shape what you see; understand filter bubbles and echo chambers; manage your digital footprint; use social media critically and constructively.
modules.readingNote: Common Sense Education — Social Media and Your Brain
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media Algorithms Control What You See
modules.practiceNote: Common Sense Education — Social Media Practice
modules.assignment: Conduct a one-week social media audit: track how much time you spend on each platform, note what types of content the algorithm promotes to you, and reflect on whether your feed accurately represents the diversity of opinions on a topic you follow.

modules.title: Artificial Intelligence Basics
modules.objectives: Define artificial intelligence, machine learning, and large language models; explain how AI tools generate output; identify AI strengths, limitations, and biases; use AI tools responsibly for learning.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How AI Works: Machine Learning Explained
modules.assignment: Use an AI writing assistant to help brainstorm ideas for an essay topic, then critically evaluate the output: identify two useful suggestions and two inaccurate or biased statements; reflect on how you would verify AI-generated content before using it.

modules.title: Digital Health & Screen Time
modules.objectives: Understand the effects of excessive screen time on sleep, attention, and mental health; set healthy digital boundaries; practice digital well-being strategies; distinguish healthy from problematic technology use.
modules.readingNote: Common Sense Education — Screen Time and Health
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Impact of Social Media on Mental Health
modules.practiceNote: Common Sense Education — Digital Health Practice
modules.assignment: Use your phone's built-in screen time tracker for one week to record daily totals by app category; identify one habit to change, set a screen time limit using your device's tools, and write a reflection after one week on the results.

modules.title: Citizenship in the Digital Age
modules.objectives: Define digital citizenship and its nine elements; understand your rights and responsibilities online; engage constructively in online communities; understand how digital actions have real-world consequences.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — What Is Digital Citizenship?
modules.practiceNote: Common Sense Education — Digital Citizenship Practice
modules.assignment: Research a recent case where someone's online behavior had real-world consequences (positive or negative); analyze what digital citizenship principles were upheld or violated and write a reflection on the lesson.

modules.title: Future of Technology
modules.objectives: Identify emerging technologies (AR/VR, blockchain, quantum computing, biotech); analyze how technology shapes work and society; develop skills for adapting to ongoing technological change.
modules.readingNote: GCFGlobal — Technology Now and in the Future
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Top 10 Future Technologies That Will Change the World
modules.assignment: Choose one emerging technology and research how it might affect a career field of your interest over the next 10–20 years; write a two-paragraph analysis explaining both the opportunities it creates and the challenges or risks it poses.

quizQuestions.question: Digital literacy is best described as:
options: The ability to type quickly
The ability to safely and effectively use digital technologies to find, evaluate, create, and communicate information
Knowing how to write computer code
Having access to a smartphone
quizQuestions.answer: The ability to safely and effectively use digital technologies to find, evaluate, create, and communicate information
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital literacy encompasses the full range of skills needed to function competently in a digital world — from finding and evaluating information to creating content and communicating responsibly.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a digital literacy skill?
options: Typing speed
Identifying a phishing email
Owning a computer
Having a social media account
quizQuestions.answer: Identifying a phishing email
quizQuestions.explanation: Identifying phishing emails requires critical evaluation of digital communication — a core digital literacy competency involving security awareness and source evaluation.

quizQuestions.question: Why is digital literacy increasingly important in today's job market?
options: All jobs now require coding skills
Most modern careers require the ability to find, evaluate, and communicate digital information effectively
Digital skills are only needed in technology companies
Employers prefer workers who avoid using technology
quizQuestions.answer: Most modern careers require the ability to find, evaluate, and communicate digital information effectively
quizQuestions.explanation: Regardless of industry, today's workplace requires digital skills — from using productivity software to evaluating online sources and communicating through digital channels.

quizQuestions.question: The 'digital divide' refers to:
options: The gap between old and new software versions
Inequality in access to and ability to use digital technologies
The difference between online and offline shopping
The speed difference between Wi-Fi and mobile data
quizQuestions.answer: Inequality in access to and ability to use digital technologies
quizQuestions.explanation: The digital divide describes unequal access to computers, internet, and digital skills across different populations — with significant implications for education and economic opportunity.

quizQuestions.question: Being a critical consumer of digital media means:
options: Complaining about digital content online
Accepting all online information as equally valid
Questioning, analyzing, and evaluating digital content before accepting it as true
Only using trusted websites you have visited before
quizQuestions.answer: Questioning, analyzing, and evaluating digital content before accepting it as true
quizQuestions.explanation: Critical media consumption involves applying questioning and analytical thinking to digital content — not accepting it at face value regardless of the source.

quizQuestions.question: HTTPS in a website address indicates:
options: The website is run by the government
The connection between your browser and the website is encrypted
The website was created recently
The website has no advertisements
quizQuestions.answer: The connection between your browser and the website is encrypted
quizQuestions.explanation: HTTPS (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure) means data transmitted between your browser and the server is encrypted — protecting information like passwords and credit card numbers.

quizQuestions.question: Phishing is a cybercrime that involves:
options: Downloading pirated software
Tricking people into revealing personal information through fake emails or websites
Using someone's Wi-Fi without permission
Sending spam messages to random addresses
quizQuestions.answer: Tricking people into revealing personal information through fake emails or websites
quizQuestions.explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails, texts, or websites that impersonate legitimate organizations to steal login credentials, financial information, or personal data.

quizQuestions.question: A trustworthy website is MOST likely to have:
options: Many pop-up advertisements
A padlock icon, clear contact information, and professional content
A very long URL with random characters
No 'About' page
quizQuestions.answer: A padlock icon, clear contact information, and professional content
quizQuestions.explanation: Trust signals include HTTPS (padlock), transparent contact information, professional design and grammar, and a clear 'About' page — all indicating a legitimate, credible site.

quizQuestions.question: Which action is SAFEST when using public Wi-Fi?
options: Checking your bank account
Using a VPN for secure browsing
Shopping with your credit card
Staying logged into all accounts
quizQuestions.answer: Using a VPN for secure browsing
quizQuestions.explanation: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) encrypts your internet traffic on public networks — preventing others on the same network from intercepting your data.

quizQuestions.question: What should you do if you receive an email from your bank asking you to click a link and enter your password?
options: Click the link immediately since banks send these routinely
Call the bank directly using a number from their official website to verify the request
Forward the email to friends as a warning
Reply to the email asking if it is legitimate
quizQuestions.answer: Call the bank directly using a number from their official website to verify the request
quizQuestions.explanation: Legitimate banks never ask for passwords via email. When in doubt, contact the institution directly through a known, verified contact method — never through links in suspicious emails.

quizQuestions.question: The SIFT method for evaluating online information stands for:
options: Search, Investigate, Filter, Trust
Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
Scan, Identify, Fact-check, Think
Source, Information, Facts, Truth
quizQuestions.answer: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
quizQuestions.explanation: SIFT is a four-step fact-checking framework: Stop (don't share immediately), Investigate the source (who made this?), Find better coverage (what do other sources say?), and Trace claims to originals.

quizQuestions.question: Lateral reading as a fact-checking strategy involves:
options: Reading an article from left to right very carefully
Opening new browser tabs to research what others say about the source or claim
Reading multiple articles on the same topic from the same website
Skimming an article quickly for key words
quizQuestions.answer: Opening new browser tabs to research what others say about the source or claim
quizQuestions.explanation: Lateral reading — leaving a site to search for information about it — is the strategy used by professional fact-checkers to quickly assess source credibility.

quizQuestions.question: Misinformation differs from disinformation in that misinformation:
options: Is always more dangerous
Is false information spread with intent to deceive
Is false or inaccurate information spread without intentional deception
Only exists on social media
quizQuestions.answer: Is false or inaccurate information spread without intentional deception
quizQuestions.explanation: Misinformation is inaccurate content spread accidentally; disinformation is deliberately false content created to deceive. Both cause harm, but intent differs.

quizQuestions.question: Clickbait headlines are designed to:
options: Summarize content accurately
Trick users into clicking by exaggerating or sensationalizing the content
Help readers decide if an article is relevant to them
Increase the educational value of the content
quizQuestions.answer: Trick users into clicking by exaggerating or sensationalizing the content
quizQuestions.explanation: Clickbait uses emotional, exaggerated, or mysterious headlines to drive clicks — often the actual content does not match the headline's implied promise.

quizQuestions.question: Which website is a reliable tool for checking the accuracy of viral claims and news stories?
options: Wikipedia
Reddit
Snopes.com
Yahoo Answers
quizQuestions.answer: Snopes.com
quizQuestions.explanation: Snopes is one of the most established fact-checking websites — it investigates viral claims, urban legends, and misinformation using primary sources and evidence.

quizQuestions.question: A 'cookie' on a website is:
options: A type of malware that damages your computer
A small text file stored by your browser that tracks your site visits and preferences
A required payment for accessing premium content
A security feature that blocks ads
quizQuestions.answer: A small text file stored by your browser that tracks your site visits and preferences
quizQuestions.explanation: Cookies store user data like login status, preferences, and browsing behavior — helpful for user experience but also used for tracking and targeted advertising.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of personal information should you NEVER share in a public online profile?
options: Your favorite TV show
Your full home address and phone number
Your general city of residence
Your educational interests
quizQuestions.answer: Your full home address and phone number
quizQuestions.explanation: Home address and phone number are sensitive personal identifiers that can be used to locate, contact, or exploit you — they should never appear in public online profiles.

quizQuestions.question: Data brokers are companies that:
options: Sell internet plans to consumers
Collect, analyze, and sell personal data about individuals to third parties
Manage data storage for businesses
Provide encryption services
quizQuestions.answer: Collect, analyze, and sell personal data about individuals to third parties
quizQuestions.explanation: Data brokers aggregate data from public records, social media, loyalty programs, and app usage to build detailed profiles on individuals — which they sell to advertisers, insurers, and others.

quizQuestions.question: A privacy policy on a website explains:
options: The website's political stance
How the website collects, uses, and shares your personal data
How much advertising the website displays
The technical specifications of the website
quizQuestions.answer: How the website collects, uses, and shares your personal data
quizQuestions.explanation: Privacy policies disclose what data is collected, why, how it is used, who it is shared with, and how long it is retained — important for understanding what you agree to.

quizQuestions.question: The best way to prevent websites from tracking your browsing across multiple sites is to:
options: Use multiple email addresses
Use a browser with enhanced tracking protection or a privacy-focused browser
Only visit websites with HTTPS
Clear your browser history once a month
quizQuestions.answer: Use a browser with enhanced tracking protection or a privacy-focused browser
quizQuestions.explanation: Browsers with tracking protection (Firefox, Brave) or features like Enhanced Tracking Protection block third-party trackers from following you across the web.

quizQuestions.question: A strong password should include:
options: Only your name and birth year for easy remembering
At least 12 characters with uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
The same password as other accounts for convenience
Only lowercase letters for fast typing
quizQuestions.answer: At least 12 characters with uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong passwords are long and complex — combining character types makes them exponentially harder to crack through brute force or dictionary attacks.

quizQuestions.question: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) improves security by:
options: Requiring a longer password
Requiring a second proof of identity (like a phone code) in addition to a password
Blocking all international login attempts
Requiring you to change passwords every day
quizQuestions.answer: Requiring a second proof of identity (like a phone code) in addition to a password
quizQuestions.explanation: MFA adds a second verification step — even if your password is stolen, attackers cannot log in without also having your phone or biometric confirmation.

quizQuestions.question: Ransomware is a type of malware that:
options: Shows you unwanted advertisements
Spies on your keystrokes
Encrypts your files and demands payment to restore access
Slows down your computer by using its processing power
quizQuestions.answer: Encrypts your files and demands payment to restore access
quizQuestions.explanation: Ransomware locks victims out of their own files by encrypting them, then demands a ransom (often in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.

quizQuestions.question: Social engineering in cybersecurity refers to:
options: Programming social media platforms
Manipulating people into revealing confidential information through psychological tactics
Building social networks for a company
Engineering software for social apps
quizQuestions.answer: Manipulating people into revealing confidential information through psychological tactics
quizQuestions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology — urgency, fear, authority, or trust — rather than technical vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access to information.

quizQuestions.question: Keeping your software and operating system updated is important for security because updates often:
options: Add new features only
Patch security vulnerabilities that hackers could exploit
Slow your computer down to prevent hacking
Reset your privacy settings
quizQuestions.answer: Patch security vulnerabilities that hackers could exploit
quizQuestions.explanation: Software updates frequently include security patches that close vulnerabilities discovered since the last version — running outdated software leaves known attack vectors open.

quizQuestions.question: In Google Sheets, which formula calculates the sum of cells A1 through A10?
options: =TOTAL(A1:A10)
=ADD(A1,A10)
=SUM(A1:A10)
=CALC(A1-A10)
quizQuestions.answer: =SUM(A1:A10)
quizQuestions.explanation: The SUM function adds a range of cells together. =SUM(A1:A10) adds all values from cell A1 to A10 — one of the most fundamental spreadsheet formulas.

quizQuestions.question: The 'comment' feature in Google Docs is most useful for:
options: Deleting sections of text
Adding private notes to a document visible only to the author
Providing feedback and questions on a shared document without changing the text
Formatting text as bold or italic
quizQuestions.answer: Providing feedback and questions on a shared document without changing the text
quizQuestions.explanation: Comments allow collaborators to annotate text, ask questions, and suggest changes without altering the original content — essential for collaborative editing.

quizQuestions.question: An effective presentation slide should:
options: Contain as much text as possible to maximize information
Use large text, minimal bullet points, and one key idea per slide
Include as many animations as possible
Have a different font style on each slide
quizQuestions.answer: Use large text, minimal bullet points, and one key idea per slide
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective slides support the speaker rather than replace them — one idea per slide with large text and visuals keeps the audience engaged without overwhelming them.

quizQuestions.question: Version history in Google Docs allows you to:
options: See the history of websites you have visited
View previous versions of a document and restore earlier drafts
See who has searched for your document online
Track your typing speed over time
quizQuestions.answer: View previous versions of a document and restore earlier drafts
quizQuestions.explanation: Google Docs automatically saves version history — you can see all changes made, when, and by whom, and restore any previous version if needed.

quizQuestions.question: When sharing a Google Doc with 'View only' permissions, recipients can:
options: Edit and delete content freely
Comment but not change the text
Read the document but not make any changes
Download and re-share the document freely
quizQuestions.answer: Read the document but not make any changes
quizQuestions.explanation: View-only permission gives access to read the document without the ability to edit, comment, or modify anything — appropriate for sharing final products.

quizQuestions.question: Netiquette refers to:
options: The technical rules for connecting to Wi-Fi networks
Etiquette and behavioral standards for online communication
The legal rules governing internet commerce
Software for managing email accounts
quizQuestions.answer: Etiquette and behavioral standards for online communication
quizQuestions.explanation: Netiquette is the informal code of conduct for respectful, appropriate behavior in digital communication — from email tone to social media behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Writing in ALL CAPS in online communication is generally interpreted as:
options: Being enthusiastic
Shouting or expressing extreme emotion
Writing a formal document
Emphasizing technical terms
quizQuestions.answer: Shouting or expressing extreme emotion
quizQuestions.explanation: All-caps text in digital communication is understood as shouting — a netiquette violation in most professional or collaborative contexts.

quizQuestions.question: A professional email should always include:
options: Multiple emojis to show personality
Memes related to the topic
A clear subject line, greeting, concise body, and professional sign-off
Attachments regardless of whether they are requested
quizQuestions.answer: A clear subject line, greeting, concise body, and professional sign-off
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional emails follow a clear structure: descriptive subject line, appropriate greeting, concise and focused body text, and a polite closing with your name.

quizQuestions.question: Cyberbullying differs from in-person bullying in that it:
options: Is less harmful because it is only online
Can reach the victim 24/7 and may involve a large audience
Only affects teenagers
Can be easily solved by turning off your computer
quizQuestions.answer: Can reach the victim 24/7 and may involve a large audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Cyberbullying's unique harms include its around-the-clock reach, potential for widespread public humiliation, and permanence of digital content — often making it harder to escape than in-person bullying.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Reply All' function in email should be used:
options: By default for every email
Only when your response is relevant to all original recipients
Whenever you want to emphasize your message
When you want to remove some recipients from the thread
quizQuestions.answer: Only when your response is relevant to all original recipients
quizQuestions.explanation: Unnecessary Reply All emails clutter inboxes and annoy recipients — use it only when your reply genuinely needs to be seen by everyone on the original thread.

quizQuestions.question: The Google search operator 'site:' allows you to:
options: Search only paid results
Limit results to a specific website or domain
Search for websites created on a specific date
Find exact word matches only
quizQuestions.answer: Limit results to a specific website or domain
quizQuestions.explanation: The site: operator (e.g., site:edu climate change) restricts search results to a specific domain — useful for searching within a particular organization or type of site.

quizQuestions.question: Google Scholar is a useful research tool because it:
options: Contains only Wikipedia articles
Searches academic journals, theses, and scholarly books
Shows only free articles from any website
Is the same as regular Google but with a different design
quizQuestions.answer: Searches academic journals, theses, and scholarly books
quizQuestions.explanation: Google Scholar indexes peer-reviewed papers, theses, court opinions, and academic books — making it far more useful than general Google for finding credible academic sources.

quizQuestions.question: Putting a phrase in quotation marks in a Google search:
options: Excludes those words from the results
Searches for the exact phrase in that specific order
Ranks results by date
Limits results to news articles only
quizQuestions.answer: Searches for the exact phrase in that specific order
quizQuestions.explanation: Quoted searches (e.g., "climate change policy") return results containing those exact words in that exact order — reducing irrelevant results.

quizQuestions.question: When using Wikipedia for academic research, the best practice is to:
options: Cite Wikipedia directly in your bibliography
Use Wikipedia as a starting point and then verify information through the cited primary sources
Avoid Wikipedia entirely as it is never accurate
Only read the article summary at the top
quizQuestions.answer: Use Wikipedia as a starting point and then verify information through the cited primary sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Wikipedia is useful for getting an overview and finding source material, but its crowd-sourced nature means it should not be cited directly — follow the footnotes to primary sources.

quizQuestions.question: A .gov domain in a web address indicates the site is:
options: A global organization website
A US government website
A website about governance and politics from any country
A website that has government approval
quizQuestions.answer: A US government website
quizQuestions.explanation: The .gov top-level domain is restricted to official US government entities — federal, state, and local agencies — making these generally credible sources for official statistics and policies.

quizQuestions.question: Copyright law protects creators by:
options: Giving the creator exclusive rights to reproduce, distribute, and display their work
Making their work available for anyone to use freely
Requiring payment to register all creative works
Protecting only commercially published works
quizQuestions.answer: Giving the creator exclusive rights to reproduce, distribute, and display their work
quizQuestions.explanation: Copyright automatically grants creators exclusive rights to their work upon creation — including reproduction, distribution, and public performance — for a limited time.

quizQuestions.question: Creative Commons licenses allow creators to:
options: Permanently give up all rights to their work
Specify exactly how others may use their work while retaining some rights
Prevent any use of their work without payment
Register their copyright with the government for free
quizQuestions.answer: Specify exactly how others may use their work while retaining some rights
quizQuestions.explanation: Creative Commons offers flexible licenses letting creators say 'some rights reserved' — specifying conditions like attribution, non-commercial use, or no derivatives.

quizQuestions.question: Fair use allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission for purposes such as:
options: Selling copies of a movie for profit
Using a full song in a commercial without credit
Quoting a paragraph from a book in a literary analysis essay
Posting an entire TV episode on YouTube
quizQuestions.answer: Quoting a paragraph from a book in a literary analysis essay
quizQuestions.explanation: Fair use permits limited use for commentary, criticism, education, and news reporting — transformative academic analysis of a small portion is typically fair use.

quizQuestions.question: The CC BY license requires users of the content to:
options: Pay the creator a royalty fee
Use the content only for non-commercial purposes
Credit the original creator (attribution)
Never modify the original work
quizQuestions.answer: Credit the original creator (attribution)
quizQuestions.explanation: CC BY (Attribution) is the most permissive Creative Commons license — users may use, modify, and share the work for any purpose as long as they credit the creator.

quizQuestions.question: To find images that are free to use for a school project, you should search:
options: Any Google Image search result
Google Images filtered for 'Creative Commons licenses' or sites like Unsplash
Instagram for popular images
Any news website for journalism photos
quizQuestions.answer: Google Images filtered for 'Creative Commons licenses' or sites like Unsplash
quizQuestions.explanation: Using Google's license filter or dedicated free-image sites (Unsplash, Pexels, Pixabay) ensures you find images explicitly licensed for reuse — avoiding copyright infringement.

quizQuestions.question: A social media filter bubble occurs when:
options: You see too many advertisements
Algorithms show you primarily content that matches your existing views, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives
You block accounts that post offensive content
Social media applies age restrictions to certain content
quizQuestions.answer: Algorithms show you primarily content that matches your existing views, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives
quizQuestions.explanation: Filter bubbles are created by recommendation algorithms that optimize for engagement — showing more of what you already like, reinforcing existing beliefs and limiting exposure to different viewpoints.

quizQuestions.question: Your digital footprint includes:
options: Only your social media posts
All data traces you leave online including posts, searches, likes, location data, and purchases
Only information on websites you created
Your saved passwords
quizQuestions.answer: All data traces you leave online including posts, searches, likes, location data, and purchases
quizQuestions.explanation: Your digital footprint is the trail of data created by your online activity — active (things you post) and passive (data collected about you) — which can persist long-term.

quizQuestions.question: Before sharing news on social media, you should:
options: Share it immediately if it confirms your beliefs
Read beyond the headline and fact-check the claim using a reliable source
Check how many likes and shares it already has
Only share content from accounts with many followers
quizQuestions.answer: Read beyond the headline and fact-check the claim using a reliable source
quizQuestions.explanation: Many people share content based on headlines alone — reading the full article and verifying the claim before sharing prevents the spread of misinformation.

quizQuestions.question: How do social media platforms primarily make money?
options: Selling physical products to users
Charging monthly subscription fees from all users
Selling targeted advertising using data about users' interests and behavior
Government grants and public funding
quizQuestions.answer: Selling targeted advertising using data about users' interests and behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Most social media platforms are free because their business model is advertising — they collect extensive data on users to enable highly targeted advertising for paying brands.

quizQuestions.question: An echo chamber in social media refers to:
options: Poor audio quality on social media videos
An online environment where people encounter only opinions that reinforce their existing beliefs
Social media groups formed around shared hobbies
The repetition of popular posts across different platforms
quizQuestions.answer: An online environment where people encounter only opinions that reinforce their existing beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Echo chambers form when people follow only accounts that share their views and algorithms amplify that content — creating environments where alternative perspectives are absent.

quizQuestions.question: Machine learning is a type of AI that works by:
options: Following a rigid set of rules programmed by humans for every situation
Learning patterns from large amounts of data to make predictions or decisions
Downloading intelligence from the internet in real time
Copying human decision-making through cameras
quizQuestions.answer: Learning patterns from large amounts of data to make predictions or decisions
quizQuestions.explanation: Machine learning algorithms find patterns in training data and use those patterns to make predictions or decisions on new data — rather than following explicit rules.

quizQuestions.question: A limitation of large language models (like chatbots) is that they can:
options: Only speak one language
Generate confident-sounding but factually incorrect information (hallucinations)
Only answer questions asked in the past 24 hours
Never produce creative writing
quizQuestions.answer: Generate confident-sounding but factually incorrect information (hallucinations)
quizQuestions.explanation: AI language models generate text based on statistical patterns, not factual verification — they can produce plausible but incorrect information, which is why output must be fact-checked.

quizQuestions.question: AI bias in an algorithm occurs when:
options: The algorithm is too slow to process requests
The AI system produces systematically unfair or discriminatory outcomes due to biased training data
The AI learns too quickly and becomes overconfident
The algorithm is programmed by people from only one country
quizQuestions.answer: The AI system produces systematically unfair or discriminatory outcomes due to biased training data
quizQuestions.explanation: AI systems trained on biased data perpetuate those biases in their outputs — which can affect hiring, lending, criminal justice, and other high-stakes decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Using an AI tool to generate an essay and submitting it as your own work is:
options: Acceptable if you edit it slightly
Academically dishonest if you do not disclose it
A smart use of technology
Required in modern education
quizQuestions.answer: Academically dishonest if you do not disclose it
quizQuestions.explanation: Submitting AI-generated work as your own without disclosure violates academic integrity policies at most institutions — it misrepresents your own understanding and effort.

quizQuestions.question: An everyday example of AI in use is:
options: A calculator doing arithmetic
A music streaming service recommending songs based on your listening history
A word processor's spell checker
A phone alarm clock
quizQuestions.answer: A music streaming service recommending songs based on your listening history
quizQuestions.explanation: Recommendation engines (Spotify, Netflix, YouTube) use machine learning to analyze your behavior and predict what you'll enjoy — a ubiquitous example of AI in daily life.

quizQuestions.question: Excessive screen time in adolescents has been linked to:
options: Improved academic performance only
Increased exercise and outdoor activity
Disrupted sleep, reduced attention span, and increased anxiety
Better reading comprehension
quizQuestions.answer: Disrupted sleep, reduced attention span, and increased anxiety
quizQuestions.explanation: Research associates high screen time in teens with delayed sleep onset (blue light suppresses melatonin), reduced attention, and higher rates of anxiety and depression.

quizQuestions.question: Blue light from screens before bedtime affects sleep because it:
options: Makes you more mentally alert so you exercise more
Suppresses melatonin production, making it harder to fall asleep
Improves your ability to dream
Has no measurable effect on sleep
quizQuestions.answer: Suppresses melatonin production, making it harder to fall asleep
quizQuestions.explanation: Blue light from LED screens suppresses melatonin — the hormone that regulates sleep — delaying sleep onset and reducing sleep quality if screens are used close to bedtime.

quizQuestions.question: A healthy digital boundary for school nights might include:
options: No screen time limits since digital skills are important for school
Putting devices away 30–60 minutes before bedtime
Keeping your phone in your bedroom for the whole night
Only using devices for social media after homework
quizQuestions.answer: Putting devices away 30–60 minutes before bedtime
quizQuestions.explanation: Creating a screen-free wind-down period before sleep allows melatonin levels to rise naturally, improving sleep quality and subsequent cognitive function.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'doom scrolling' refers to:
options: Scrolling through gaming content for hours
Compulsively consuming large amounts of negative news or distressing content online
Accidentally deleting important files
Scrolling too fast through social media
quizQuestions.answer: Compulsively consuming large amounts of negative news or distressing content online
quizQuestions.explanation: Doom scrolling is the tendency to continue reading negative news or alarming social media content despite the distress it causes — driven by anxiety and the algorithm's negativity bias.

quizQuestions.question: Digital well-being tools built into smartphones typically allow users to:
options: Increase their data plan speed
View screen time statistics and set app usage limits
Block all internet access permanently
Delete all social media accounts at once
quizQuestions.answer: View screen time statistics and set app usage limits
quizQuestions.explanation: Built-in screen time dashboards (Screen Time on iOS, Digital Wellbeing on Android) show usage patterns by app and allow users to set daily limits — supporting mindful technology use.

quizQuestions.question: Digital citizenship includes which of the following responsibilities?
options: Using technology only for personal entertainment
Respecting others online, protecting privacy, and thinking critically about digital information
Maximizing your screen time for productivity
Avoiding all social media platforms
quizQuestions.answer: Respecting others online, protecting privacy, and thinking critically about digital information
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital citizenship involves the ethical, responsible use of technology — including online respect, privacy protection, critical thinking, and understanding digital rights and responsibilities.

quizQuestions.question: Your online reputation matters because:
options: Online posts disappear after 24 hours
Digital content can persist and be found by future employers, schools, and others
Only people you know follow your social media accounts
Social media accounts are completely private by default
quizQuestions.answer: Digital content can persist and be found by future employers, schools, and others
quizQuestions.explanation: Content posted online can persist long-term — screenshots, caches, and archives mean things you post now can affect your reputation, college admissions, and employment years later.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of being a positive digital citizen?
options: Sharing a friend's private photo without permission
Reporting harassment you witness in an online gaming community
Spreading a rumor because many people are already sharing it
Creating an anonymous account to post negative comments
quizQuestions.answer: Reporting harassment you witness in an online gaming community
quizQuestions.explanation: Being an 'upstander' — reporting or speaking out against harassment — is a core element of positive digital citizenship, using your digital presence constructively.

quizQuestions.question: Before posting something online, a good digital citizenship question to ask is:
options: Will this get the most likes?
Is this legal, ethical, and something I would be comfortable with anyone seeing?
Has someone else already posted this?
Will this make me seem funny?
quizQuestions.answer: Is this legal, ethical, and something I would be comfortable with anyone seeing?
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'Would I be comfortable if everyone could see this?' test helps prevent sharing content that could be harmful, embarrassing, or inappropriate when viewed by parents, teachers, or future employers.

quizQuestions.question: The right to be forgotten (digital right to erasure) refers to:
options: The government's ability to erase all data from the internet
An individual's right to request that their personal data be deleted from online databases
Forgetting passwords and requesting a reset
Technology that automatically erases old posts
quizQuestions.answer: An individual's right to request that their personal data be deleted from online databases
quizQuestions.explanation: In many jurisdictions (including the EU under GDPR), individuals have a 'right to be forgotten' — the right to request that organizations erase their personal data.

quizQuestions.question: Augmented reality (AR) differs from virtual reality (VR) in that AR:
options: Creates a completely immersive virtual environment
Overlays digital information onto the real world
Requires a full-body suit to operate
Can only be experienced through special glasses
quizQuestions.answer: Overlays digital information onto the real world
quizQuestions.explanation: AR adds digital elements (images, text, data) onto a real-world view (like Pokémon Go or Snapchat filters). VR creates a fully immersive digital environment replacing the real world.

quizQuestions.question: Which skill will be most important for workers adapting to ongoing technological change?
options: Mastering one specific software tool permanently
Lifelong learning, adaptability, and the ability to learn new technologies quickly
Avoiding all new technologies until they are established
Only using technologies older than five years
quizQuestions.answer: Lifelong learning, adaptability, and the ability to learn new technologies quickly
quizQuestions.explanation: As technology evolves rapidly, the ability to continuously learn new tools and adapt to change is more valuable than deep mastery of any single tool that may become obsolete.

quizQuestions.question: Blockchain technology is primarily notable for:
options: Making websites load faster
Providing a decentralized, tamper-resistant way to record and verify transactions
Replacing all passwords with biometric data
Enabling faster wireless internet connections
quizQuestions.answer: Providing a decentralized, tamper-resistant way to record and verify transactions
quizQuestions.explanation: Blockchain creates a distributed ledger of transactions that is extremely difficult to alter — the underlying technology of cryptocurrencies and many emerging decentralized applications.

quizQuestions.question: Automation and AI are most likely to displace jobs that involve:
options: Creative problem-solving and interpersonal skills
Routine, repetitive tasks that follow predictable patterns
Strategic leadership and ethical judgment
Physical work requiring fine motor skills in complex environments
quizQuestions.answer: Routine, repetitive tasks that follow predictable patterns
quizQuestions.explanation: Jobs involving predictable, repetitive processes are most vulnerable to automation. Jobs requiring creativity, social intelligence, and complex judgment are more resistant.

quizQuestions.question: The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to:
options: A network connecting only computers and smartphones
The global network of everyday physical devices connected to the internet and each other
A new faster version of the internet
Social media platforms connecting people worldwide
quizQuestions.answer: The global network of everyday physical devices connected to the internet and each other
quizQuestions.explanation: IoT describes the growing network of connected devices — from smart thermostats and watches to industrial sensors — that collect and exchange data via the internet.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a phishing attack?
options: Hacking into a network by exploiting software vulnerabilities
Deceiving people through fake messages or websites to steal personal information
Installing malware by connecting a USB drive
Overloading a server with traffic until it crashes
questions.answer: Deceiving people through fake messages or websites to steal personal information
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack that uses deceptive communication — fake emails, websites, or texts — to trick victims into revealing credentials or financial information.

questions.question: The SIFT method advises you to 'Investigate the source' by:
options: Reading the article very carefully from beginning to end
Looking up who made the content and whether they are credible before reading further
Counting the number of sources cited in the article
Checking how many social media shares the article has
questions.answer: Looking up who made the content and whether they are credible before reading further
questions.explanation: Investigating the source means quickly checking who is behind the content before investing time reading it — a key habit that prevents being misled by unreliable sources.

questions.question: Adjusting your privacy settings on social media platforms helps to:
options: Increase the number of followers you have
Control who can see your personal information and content
Improve the quality of your internet connection
Prevent the platform from making money from advertising
questions.answer: Control who can see your personal information and content
questions.explanation: Privacy settings let you decide who can see your profile, posts, location, and other personal data — an essential step in managing your digital footprint.

questions.question: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is most important because:
options: It makes logging in faster
Even if your password is stolen, a second factor prevents unauthorized access
It eliminates the need for passwords entirely
It works only on mobile devices
questions.answer: Even if your password is stolen, a second factor prevents unauthorized access
questions.explanation: MFA's key benefit is protection after password compromise — requiring a second verification step (code, biometric) means stolen passwords alone cannot unlock your account.

questions.question: In Google Sheets, which formula would calculate the average of cells B2 through B20?
options: =MEAN(B2:B20)
=AVERAGE(B2:B20)
=AVG(B2:B20)
questions.answer: =AVERAGE(B2:B20)
questions.explanation: The AVERAGE function calculates the arithmetic mean of a range. =AVERAGE(B2:B20) sums all values and divides by the count automatically.

questions.question: Lateral reading is a fact-checking strategy used by professional fact-checkers that involves:
options: Carefully reading every word of an article before forming an opinion
Immediately searching for information about the source from other websites
Reading only the left column of a Wikipedia article
Comparing the article to other articles on the same website
questions.answer: Immediately searching for information about the source from other websites
questions.explanation: Lateral reading means leaving a website to quickly check what other credible sources say about it — far more efficient than reading an unreliable site in detail.

questions.question: A Creative Commons CC BY-NC license allows others to:
options: Use the work commercially without credit
Use and modify the work non-commercially with attribution
Use the work for any purpose without any restrictions
Create derivative works and sell them
questions.answer: Use and modify the work non-commercially with attribution
questions.explanation: CC BY-NC means users must credit the creator (BY) and may only use the work for non-commercial (NC) purposes — a common license for educational content.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a passive digital footprint?
options: A photo you post on Instagram
A comment you leave on a YouTube video
Your location data collected by an app you use
An email you send to a friend
questions.answer: Your location data collected by an app you use
questions.explanation: A passive digital footprint is data collected about you without your direct input — location data, browsing behavior tracked by cookies, app usage statistics.

questions.question: A netiquette rule that applies to professional email communication is:
options: Always use lowercase letters for a casual tone
Include a descriptive subject line and professional greeting
Send emails at any time of day without considering the recipient's time zone
Use emojis to show friendliness in all professional contexts
questions.answer: Include a descriptive subject line and professional greeting
questions.explanation: A clear subject line helps recipients prioritize and find emails; a professional greeting sets the appropriate tone — both fundamental to effective professional email.

questions.question: The Google search operator that finds pages that link to a specific URL is:
options: site:
link:
inurl:
related:
questions.answer: link:
questions.explanation: The link: operator finds pages that contain links to a specific URL — useful for researching what sites reference a particular page.

questions.question: Social media algorithms primarily show you content that:
options: Is posted by accounts you have never interacted with
Maximizes engagement based on your past behavior
Is the most factually accurate available
Was posted most recently with no other factors
questions.answer: Maximizes engagement based on your past behavior
questions.explanation: Social media algorithms optimize for engagement (likes, shares, time spent) — showing content similar to what you have previously interacted with, regardless of its accuracy or diversity.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST secure approach to password management?
options: Using the same strong password for all accounts to avoid forgetting
Writing all passwords in a notebook kept at your desk
Using a reputable password manager to generate and store unique passwords for each account
Changing all passwords to your birthday for easy recall
questions.answer: Using a reputable password manager to generate and store unique passwords for each account
questions.explanation: A password manager creates strong, unique passwords for every account and encrypts them securely — addressing both the need for complexity and the challenge of remembering many credentials.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the concept of a filter bubble?
options: A browser extension that blocks offensive content
An algorithm-driven information environment where users see only content matching their existing views
A cybersecurity tool that filters malicious emails
A privacy feature that hides your location from apps
questions.answer: An algorithm-driven information environment where users see only content matching their existing views
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles are created by personalization algorithms that limit information exposure — users see increasingly narrow, confirmatory content rather than a diverse range of perspectives.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of misinformation as opposed to disinformation?
options: Misinformation is always more widely shared
Misinformation is spread without intent to deceive, while disinformation is deliberately false
Misinformation only occurs on social media platforms
Misinformation is always created by governments
questions.answer: Misinformation is spread without intent to deceive, while disinformation is deliberately false
questions.explanation: The distinction is intent: misinformation is false content shared accidentally (by people who believe it or didn't check); disinformation is deliberately created to manipulate.

questions.question: Being a responsible digital citizen when you encounter cyberbullying means:
options: Participating if most of your friends are involved
Ignoring it because it is not your problem
Reporting it to the platform and, if appropriate, to a trusted adult
Retaliating against the bully online
questions.answer: Reporting it to the platform and, if appropriate, to a trusted adult
questions.explanation: Digital citizenship involves being an upstander — using reporting tools to flag harassment and seeking adult help for serious cases, rather than participating or ignoring it.

questions.question: A ransomware attack can BEST be prevented by:
options: Using a very long password
Regularly backing up data to an offline location and keeping software updated
Turning off your computer when not in use
Only using websites with HTTPS
questions.answer: Regularly backing up data to an offline location and keeping software updated
questions.explanation: Regular offline backups mean ransomware cannot destroy your only copy of data; software updates close vulnerabilities ransomware exploits — the two most effective defenses.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about AI language models is TRUE?
options: They always produce factually accurate information
They cannot generate creative writing
They generate text based on statistical patterns and can produce plausible but incorrect content
They have real-time access to all internet information
questions.answer: They generate text based on statistical patterns and can produce plausible but incorrect content
questions.explanation: LLMs generate text by predicting likely word sequences based on training data — they do not 'know' facts and can generate convincingly written but incorrect information (hallucinations).

questions.question: Which type of tracking would continue even if you deleted all your browser cookies?
options: Session cookies
Browser fingerprinting, which identifies your device by its unique configuration
First-party cookies
Third-party cookies
questions.answer: Browser fingerprinting, which identifies your device by its unique configuration
questions.explanation: Browser fingerprinting tracks users through unique combinations of browser settings, installed fonts, screen resolution, and other attributes — working even without cookies.

questions.question: When evaluating the 'Currency' of a source using the CRAAP test, you are assessing:
options: How much the article cost to produce
Whether the information is recent enough for your research topic
Whether the source accepts payment for publishing
How popular the source is currently
questions.answer: Whether the information is recent enough for your research topic
questions.explanation: Currency evaluates the date of information — critical for fast-changing fields like medicine or technology where old information may be incorrect.

questions.question: The Internet of Things (IoT) raises which privacy concern?
options: IoT devices make the internet slower for everyone
Connected devices continuously collect and transmit data about your behavior and environment
IoT devices can only be used with special permissions
IoT increases costs for internet providers
questions.answer: Connected devices continuously collect and transmit data about your behavior and environment
questions.explanation: IoT devices (smart speakers, thermostats, health trackers) constantly gather and transmit data about daily behavior — raising significant questions about who has access and how it is used.

questions.question: Which of the following is an ethical use of an AI writing tool for a school assignment?
options: Submitting the AI's output verbatim as your essay
Using AI to generate practice questions to test yourself, then writing your own response
Having AI write your entire paper and changing a few words
Using AI to translate your assignment from another language you wrote it in to avoid a language class
questions.answer: Using AI to generate practice questions to test yourself, then writing your own response
questions.explanation: Using AI as a study tool — generating questions, explanations, or feedback — supports your own learning without misrepresenting your work as something you didn't produce.

questions.question: The 'Purpose' criterion in the CRAAP test evaluates:
options: Whether the article has a clear purpose statement
Why the information was created — to inform, persuade, sell, entertain, or manipulate
Whether the article is long enough to be useful
How many purposes the article serves
questions.answer: Why the information was created — to inform, persuade, sell, entertain, or manipulate
questions.explanation: Understanding the purpose behind content reveals potential bias — advertising content, advocacy websites, and satire all have different motivations that affect how their information should be interpreted.

questions.question: Fair use is most likely to apply when you:
options: Post an entire movie on YouTube for free
Quote two sentences from a published book to support an argument in your own essay
Sell printed copies of a copyrighted photograph
Create a website using a copyrighted logo without permission
questions.answer: Quote two sentences from a published book to support an argument in your own essay
questions.explanation: Fair use typically applies to small amounts of copyrighted material used for commentary, criticism, or education — transformative academic use of brief quotations is the clearest example.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST strategy for maintaining digital well-being?
options: Avoiding all technology except for school assignments
Setting intentional boundaries around technology use while maintaining engagement for meaningful purposes
Spending unlimited time on social media since it keeps you connected
Only using technology when other people are also using it
questions.answer: Setting intentional boundaries around technology use while maintaining engagement for meaningful purposes
questions.explanation: Digital well-being is not about eliminating technology but using it intentionally — balancing benefits (learning, connection, productivity) with boundaries that protect sleep, attention, and mental health.

questions.question: When using Google Scholar to find sources, you should prioritize articles that are:
options: The most recently cited articles regardless of their content
Peer-reviewed and published in reputable academic journals
Only available as free full-text downloads
Written by the most famous authors in the field
questions.answer: Peer-reviewed and published in reputable academic journals
questions.explanation: Peer review is the key quality indicator for academic sources — it means the work was evaluated by independent subject-matter experts before publication.

questions.question: Which practice BEST reduces the spread of misinformation online?
options: Only sharing content from accounts you follow
Pausing before sharing, verifying the claim from independent sources, and sharing responsibly
Sharing widely to let others decide what is true
Trusting content that aligns with your existing knowledge
questions.answer: Pausing before sharing, verifying the claim from independent sources, and sharing responsibly
questions.explanation: Most misinformation spreads because people share without checking. Pausing and verifying — even briefly — dramatically reduces your role in spreading false information.

questions.question: A positive digital footprint can benefit you by:
options: Preventing any organization from finding information about you
Showcasing your skills, achievements, and responsible online behavior to future employers or colleges
Making your social media accounts visible to more followers
Reducing the amount of data companies can collect about you
questions.answer: Showcasing your skills, achievements, and responsible online behavior to future employers or colleges
questions.explanation: A curated positive digital footprint — portfolios, thoughtful posts, professional profiles — can strengthen college applications and job searches by demonstrating character and competence.

questions.question: Automation's impact on the job market is BEST described as:
options: Eliminating all jobs within the next decade
Transforming many jobs by automating routine tasks while creating new roles requiring human skills
Only affecting manufacturing jobs
Having no effect on professional or white-collar jobs
questions.answer: Transforming many jobs by automating routine tasks while creating new roles requiring human skills
questions.explanation: History shows automation eliminates some tasks while creating new types of work — the net effect depends on how quickly workers and education systems adapt.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most effective defense against social engineering attacks?
options: Installing the latest antivirus software
Educating users to recognize and verify suspicious requests before complying
Using very complex passwords
Never using email for communication
questions.answer: Educating users to recognize and verify suspicious requests before complying
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities — education and a habit of verification (calling back on known numbers, checking with supervisors) are the primary defenses.

questions.question: Digital citizenship responsibilities include:
options: Sharing as much personal information as possible to build your online presence
Respecting intellectual property, protecting your privacy, and treating others with respect online
Maximizing your time online to stay current
Sharing any information that seems important, regardless of its accuracy
questions.answer: Respecting intellectual property, protecting your privacy, and treating others with respect online
questions.explanation: The nine elements of digital citizenship include digital rights and responsibilities, ethics, security, privacy, and literacy — all centered on informed, ethical, and respectful online participation.

questions.question: Deepfakes pose a particular risk to society because they:
options: Make movies look more realistic
Use AI to create convincing fake images or videos of real people, potentially spreading disinformation
Require expensive equipment that limits their production
Can only be created by professional filmmakers
questions.answer: Use AI to create convincing fake images or videos of real people, potentially spreading disinformation
questions.explanation: Deepfakes can falsely portray real people saying or doing things they never did — with potential to damage reputations, spread political disinformation, and undermine trust in video evidence.

questions.question: Which presentation design principle improves audience comprehension?
options: Using as many different fonts as possible for variety
Including all talking points in full sentences on each slide
Using visuals and minimal text to complement — not replace — the speaker's words
Filling every slide completely to show thoroughness
questions.answer: Using visuals and minimal text to complement — not replace — the speaker's words
questions.explanation: Slides are visual aids, not scripts. Audiences cannot read dense slides and listen simultaneously — minimal text with supporting visuals enhances rather than competes with the presentation.

questions.question: The 'right to be forgotten' is most associated with which value?
options: Freedom of speech online
The right to have outdated or harmful personal data removed from online databases
The right to anonymous online communication
The right to copy any content you find online
questions.answer: The right to have outdated or harmful personal data removed from online databases
questions.explanation: The right to be forgotten (erasure) allows individuals to request removal of personal data that is no longer relevant or is causing harm — recognized in GDPR and growing globally.

questions.question: Which skill is considered MOST future-proof as technology continues to advance?
options: Proficiency in one specific programming language
The ability to quickly learn new tools and adapt to technological change
Memorizing technical specifications of current devices
Only working in jobs that cannot be computerized
questions.answer: The ability to quickly learn new tools and adapt to technological change
questions.explanation: In a rapidly changing technological landscape, adaptability and continuous learning are the most durable skills — specific tool knowledge may become obsolete while the ability to learn remains valuable.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST summarizes the responsibility of digital literacy for democracy?
options: Digital literacy is only important for personal safety
Critically evaluating online information is essential for making informed civic and political decisions
Government agencies should decide what information citizens can access online
Digital literacy skills are only necessary for people who use social media
questions.answer: Critically evaluating online information is essential for making informed civic and political decisions
questions.explanation: Democracy depends on informed citizens — as more civic discourse and political information moves online, the ability to evaluate sources and resist manipulation becomes essential for democratic participation.
